coredata.dc:title: Ab initio study of graphitic-N and pyridinic-N doped graphene for catalytic oxygen reduction reactions
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Han
Sit, Patrick H.-L.
coredata.dc:description: The nitrogen doped graphene has been reported to exhibit good catalytic activity for the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) and to be a promising material to replace the use of platinum as the ORR catalyst in fuel cell application. Yet, the roles of the different doping types, e.g. the pyridinic-N and graphitic-N dopants, in mediating the ORR are still controversial. Such understanding is of great importance for the development of highly active ORR catalysts. In this work, we perform density functional theory (DFT) study of the ORR processes on the pyridinic-N and graphitic-N doped graphene. In the study of the doped graphene quantum dots, we found that the binding strength of O2 on the graphene correlates closely with electron donation from the graphene to the O2. The presence of the graphitic-N dopants is found to facilitate the O2 binding, and thus the ORR reaction, as each graphitic-N dopant introduces one extra electron to the graphene. We then extend our study to the graphitic-N doped extended graphene to examine the effects of the dopant percentage on the ORR process. As the graphitic-N dopant percentage increases, the difference between the energy of the lowest spin state and the energy of the second lowest spin state decreases, which favors the transfer of electrons to the O2 and thus the binding. On the other hand, according to the energy profiles of the ORR reaction, the ORR processes could stop at the unfavorable reduction of *O to *OH on the extended graphene. However, after the binding of a second O2, we identify two possible ORR pathways. One of them is the continued reduction of the *O in the presence of the newly adsorbed *O2. The other one involves the reduction of the newly adsorbed *O2 in the presence of the *O. Our findings contribute to the understanding of ORR processes on N-doped graphene and would help to the development of efficient ORR catalysts.

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authlast highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: ORR processes on N-doped graphene dots
Adsorption of O2 on N-doped graphene dots
ORR mechanisms of the N-doped graphene dots
ORR processes on the graphitic-N doped extended graphene
ORR processes on the uniformly doped extended graphene
ORR processes on non-uniformly doped extended graphene

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The structures of the graphene quantum dot (A), extended graphene with 72 carbon atoms (B) and extended graphene with 96 carbon atoms (C). Carbons are gray spheres and hydrogen are white spheres.

The N-doped graphene quantum dot models considered in this work. 1N and 3N denote the number of nitrogen atoms doped on the graphene. P and G represent the pyridinic-N and graphitic-N doping respectively. E and M mean that nitrogen atoms substitute the carbon atoms at edge sites and non-edge sites. ‘c’ and ‘s’ in the parenthesis denote whether the dopants are clustered together (c) or spread-out (s). Gray, blue and white spheres represent the C, N and H atoms respectively. 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 and 6 are the indices of the carbon atoms considered for O2 adsorption.
All the O2 binding sites (in red circles) found on the N-doped graphene quantum dots. Gray, blue and white spheres represent the C, N and H atoms respectively.
The ORR processes on 3N-G-M at Site 1 (A), on 3N-G-E(s) at Site 3 (B) and on 3N-G-E(c) at Site 5 (C).
The doping structures of 1N (1.4%), 4N (5.6%) and 9N (12.5%) and the chosen sites for O2 binding. Gray and blue spheres represent the C and N atoms.
The ORR processes at Site 6 on 9N (Path-1), the reduction of *O with the existence of second *O2 binding (Path-2a) and the ORR processes with the existence of *O (Path-2b).

The non-uniform doping structures of extended graphene of different nitrogen percentage, 3N (3.1%), 4N′ (4.2%), 6N (6.3%) and 8N (8.3%). 4N′ is used to distinguish it from the uniform doping structure 4N. ‘a′’ denotes this site is equivalent to site ‘a’ and we only calculated the binding energy and CO distance at site ‘a’. Gray and blue spheres represent the C and N atoms.
Reaction schemes of ORR on extended N-doped graphene: (A) the presence of *O2 helps the reduction of *O; (B) the new bonded O2 is reduced to two water molecules with the existence of *O binding.

The adsorption energy (in kcal/mol) and C–O distances (Å) of O2 at the different carbon sites. a For the cases that the O2 adsorbs in the side-on configuration, two C–O distances are reported. The first value corresponds to the C of the site considered and the value in the parenthesis corresponds to another C with the shortest distance.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: ‘–’ denotes that the O2 does not locate at the chosen sites in the optimized structure.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The O2 binding energies (in kcal/mol), CO bond orders and the magnetizations on O2 after binding. a.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: On 3N-G-E(s) and 3N-G-M, the three graphitic-N are equal. Here, we only pick one graphitic-N and studied the O2 binding. And on 3N-G-M, Site 1 and Site 4 are equal. We only pick Site 1 for calculation.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The O2 adsorption energy (in kcal/mol) and CO distance (Å) at each site on 1N, 4N and 9N.

head.ce:title: Ab initio study of graphitic-N and pyridinic-N doped graphene for catalytic oxygen reduction reactions

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Energy and Environment, City University of Hong Kong, Kowloon, Hong Kong Special Administrative Region
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Energy and Environment, City University of Hong Kong, Kowloon, Hong Kong Special Administrative Region

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The nitrogen doped graphene has recently attracted great attention due to its good catalytic activity for the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) in fuel cell application. However, the differences between the most studied N-dopant configurations on the graphene, pyridinic-N and graphitic-N, are still unclear. This work studies the roles they play in determining O2 binding and catalyzing the ORR processes, and identifies two possible ORR pathways.

The nitrogen doped graphene has been reported to exhibit good catalytic activity for the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) and to be a promising material to replace the use of platinum as the ORR catalyst in fuel cell application. Yet, the roles of the different doping types, e.g. the pyridinic-N and graphitic-N dopants, in mediating the ORR are still controversial. Such understanding is of great importance for the development of highly active ORR catalysts. In this work, we perform density functional theory (DFT) study of the ORR processes on the pyridinic-N and graphitic-N doped graphene. In the study of the doped graphene quantum dots, we found that the binding strength of O2 on the graphene correlates closely with electron donation from the graphene to the O2. The presence of the graphitic-N dopants is found to facilitate the O2 binding, and thus the ORR reaction, as each graphitic-N dopant introduces one extra electron to the graphene. We then extend our study to the graphitic-N doped extended graphene to examine the effects of the dopant percentage on the ORR process. As the graphitic-N dopant percentage increases, the difference between the energy of the lowest spin state and the energy of the second lowest spin state decreases, which favors the transfer of electrons to the O2 and thus the binding. On the other hand, according to the energy profiles of the ORR reaction, the ORR processes could stop at the unfavorable reduction of *O to *OH on the extended graphene. However, after the binding of a second O2, we identify two possible ORR pathways. One of them is the continued reduction of the *O in the presence of the newly adsorbed *O2. The other one involves the reduction of the newly adsorbed *O2 in the presence of the *O. Our findings contribute to the understanding of ORR processes on N-doped graphene and would help to the development of efficient ORR catalysts.

ce:section.ce:para: Fuel cells are promising technology to tackle the rising global energy demand and environment issues caused by the use of fossil fuels. It generates electricity by electrochemically reducing O2 and oxidizing fuels like hydrogen to form water as the byproduct. Till now, this energy conversion technology has received great attention due to its potential high energy conversion efficiency and the high energy storage capacity of the hydrogen fuel with little environmental impact. Fuel cells are expected to be applicable in different areas including transportation, stationary, and portable power sources. Despite the promising potentials, the sluggish oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) kinetics on the cathode requires the use of efficient catalysts. Traditionally, platinum (Pt)-based materials have been regarded as the best ORR catalysts. However, Pt is rare and is susceptible to CO deactivation.[1] This greatly hinders the prospect of fuel cells for widespread applications. Thus, exploration of alternative catalysts to replace/reduce the use of Pt is crucial.

Recently the nitrogen-doped graphene has attracted attention as an alternative to the Pt-based materials due to their high ORR activity and long-term operation stability.[2–8] However, many issues including the properties of the catalytic sites and the effects of different dopant configurations on the catalytic performance remain elusive. In fact, different possible doping configurations, e.g. pyridinic-N, graphitic-N, pyrrolic-N, nitrile, –NH2, interstitial N, co-exist in the nitrogen-doped graphene.[9,10] Some works attributed the superior ORR catalytic activities to the pyridinic-N and pyrrolic-N dopants.[2,11,12] However, other study showed that the pyridinic-N doped graphene exhibits a low ORR activity, on which the O2 is majorly reduced through a 2e− pathway.[8] Therefore, the pyridinic-N dopants may not be the key promoter for the ORR activity. On the other hand, it has been found that the graphene with a large amount of graphitic-N dopants exhibit a higher ORR activity than that with a large amount of pyridinic-N dopants.[13] Moreover, Wang et al. suggests that the introduction of nitrogen, especially graphitic-N, to the graphene facilitates the transfer of electrons from the carbons to the antibonding orbitals of O2.[14] Lai et al. reported that the electrocatalytic activity of the N-doped graphene is dependent on the amount of the graphitic-N dopants while the pyridinic-N improves the onset potential for ORR.[7] In summary, although many works have reported that the N-doped graphene exhibits promising ORR catalytic activity, the nature of the active sites and the dopants involved are still controversial.

In this regard, simulations are powerful tools to provide fundamental understanding of this class of catalysts.[5,10,15–20] Yu et al. performed density functional theory (DFT) study of the ORR mechanism on the graphene doped with nitrogen at the doping concentration of 8.33%. They found that the associative adsorption mechanism was energetically more favorable than the dissociative mechanism.[17] Kim et al. studied the adsorption of O2 on eleven model doped-graphene structures and reported that the graphene doped with the graphitic-N dopants at the edges exhibit higher ORR activity.[21] Li et al. simulated the ORR and oxygen evolution reaction (OER) on the N-doped graphene and found that edge doping reduced the overpotential and enhanced the ORR/OER catalytic activity.[22] Other kinds of defects have also been studied, such as the Stone-Wales (SW) defect. Shan et al. reported that SW defect facilitates the adsorption of O2 on the N-doped graphene[16] and Zhang et al. suggested that the spin density of the carbon atoms is a factor determining the O2 binding position while charge would enhance the attractive force.[23] Till now, many studies have focused on understanding the ORR mechanism on the N-doped graphene. But the nature of the active sites on the N-doped graphene remains unclear and the origin of ORR activity is still not clear known. Addressing these issues will provide useful guide to the design and development of highly efficient ORR catalysts.

In this work, we used DFT techniques to probe the electronic properties of the N-doped graphene and simulate the catalytic ORR processes. Our computational study provides new insights important for the development of efficient electrocatalysts for fuel cells. In Section 2, we provide the simulation details. We then discuss our results in Section 3. We first studied the binding of O2, which can be regarded as the first step of the ORR processes, on the graphene quantum dots. Then we selected the most favorable O2 binding sites as the catalytic site to simulate the ORR processes. Later, we extended our study from the graphene quantum dots to the extended graphene sheets. The graphene sheets were doped with different number of graphitic-N dopants to explore the effects of doping percentage on O2 binding. After that, we simulated the ORR processes on the extended graphene and identified possible mechanisms in which O2 is reduced through a 4e− pathway. Lastly, we conclude our work in Section 4.

The density functional theory (DFT)[24,25] calculations were performed with the QUANTUM ESPRESSO[26] package using the Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE)[27] exchange–correlation functional and the ultrasoft scalar relativistic pseudopotentials.[28] Spin-polarization calculation was made for all the calculations. The van der Waals (vdw) correction of Grimme et al. was considered to account for the dispersion interactions.[29,30] Planewave basis sets with the cutoff energies of 30 and 240 Ry were respectively used for the wavefunctions and the augmented part of the charge density.

We first simulated the ORR processes on graphene quantum dots which contain 54 carbon atoms. Both the graphitic-N and pyridinic-N dopants were studied. For this quantum dot model, a cubic simulation cell of 20.0 Å × 20.0 Å × 20.0 Å in size is adopted. The edge carbon atoms are terminated with hydrogen atoms (Fig. 1 A). Then we extended our study to the extended graphene. We considered two models for the extended graphene. In the first model, a single-layer extended graphene of 72 carbon atoms is situated in a hexagonal simulation cell of 14.76 Å × 14.76 Å × 20.00 Å (Fig. 1B). This model allows us to consider graphenes uniformly doped with the graphitic-N dopants. However, due to the limited number of uniform doping structures, we also studied the single-layer extended graphene with 96 carbon atoms, on which the graphitic-N dopants are arranged in a regular way. The graphene is situated in the orthorhombic simulation cell of 14.76 Å× 17.05 Å × 20.00 Å (Fig. 1C). For all the simulations, the reciprocal space was sampled at the gamma point which is adequate for the studies in this work.

The water solvent effects were considered using the Conductor-like Screening MOdel (COSMO) solvation model of the dielectric constant of 78.3.[31] The adsorption energies of O2 on the N-doped graphene were calculated according to the following equation: ΔE ads = E (adsorbates/surface) − E (adsorbates) − E (surface), where E (surface), E (adsorbates), and E (adsorbates/surface) are the total energy of the N-doped graphene, the O2 and the graphene adsorbed with an O2, respectively. The reaction energy of each ORR step was calculated with respect to the hydrogen electrode. For each ORR step, we converted the calculated DFT reaction energy (ΔE) to the Gibbs free energy (ΔG) by adding the zero-point energy (ZPE) and entropic (TS) corrections to the solvated phase molecules and ZPE corrections to the adsorbed intermediates adopted from Ref. 32 and 33, as listed in Table S1 in the Supporting Information (SI). ΔG was calculated according to the following expression:
(1) ΔG = ΔE + ΔZPE − TΔS + ΔG pH

ΔG pH is the correction of the H+ free energy by the concentration dependence of entropy: ΔG pH = kTln[H+] = −pH·kT ln10. Here, we simulated the ORR processes in the acidic environment and the pH was set to 0, which corresponds to the H+ concentration of 1 mol·L−1. In this case, ΔG pH equals 0 eV. Thus, ΔG is simplified as ΔG = ΔE + ΔZPE – TΔS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ORR processes on N-doped graphene dots
ce:section.ce:para: We first studied the ORR processes on the N-doped graphene quantum dots. In this work, we focused on the study of graphitic-N and pyridinic-N dopants. Other dopant types, like pyrrole, were not considered. To determine the differences between the graphitic-N (G) and pyridinic-N (P) dopants, we considered graphene doped with the same type of dopants, i.e. either graphitic-N only or pyridinic-N only. We have also considered the effect of the number of dopants, i.e. one nitrogen (1N) and three nitrogen (3N), as well as the dopant location, i.e. at the edge site and the non-edge site (denoted as E and M respectively). For example, 1N-P-E means that there is one pyridinic-N replacing the edge site carbon atom. 3N-G-M means that there are three graphitic-N dopants replacing three non-edge carbon atoms. Beside the dopant type and dopant number, we have also taken the arrangement of the dopants into consideration. For the graphene doped with three graphitic-N dopants, we have considered two doping models, 1) dopants clustered together (c) and 2) dopant being spread-out (s). Fig. 2 shows the list of doping configurations in the graphene quantum dot studied.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption of O2 on N-doped graphene dots
ce:section.ce:para: O2 binding can be considered to be the first step of the ORR process. It has been reported that O2 prefers to bind to the carbon atoms adjacent to the nitrogen atoms.[23] As shown in Fig. 2, we selected six carbon atoms close to the nitrogen atoms for each structure as the possible binding sites to study. On 3N-G-E(s) and 3N-G-M, the three graphitic-N dopants are equivalent, and so only the C atoms around one of the nitrogen atoms are considered. The corresponding adsorption energies at each site are listed in Table 1 . Based on the energy, the adsorption of O2 on the graphitic-N doped graphene is generally more favorable than that on the pyridinic-N doped graphene. For example, the most favorable site on 1N-G-E is Site 6, with the adsorption energy of −16.1 kcal/mol. While the most negative adsorption energy on 1N-P-E is −3.6 kcal/mol at Site 4. Although the adsorption energy of O2 at Site 6 on 1N-G-E is −16.1 kcal/mol, the CO distance is 2.22 Å, and the CO bond order is 0.259. Therefore, the interaction between the O2 and 1N-G-E is a non-bonding interaction. On the other hand, the adsorption energy becomes more negative as the number of the graphitic-N dopants increases. The adsorption energy becomes −38.6 kcal/mol at Site 5 on 3N-G-E(c) and −29.8 kcal/mol at Site 3 on 3N-G-E(s). The CO distance becomes 1.41 Å and 1.43 Å, respectively. A similar trend has also been found on the graphene with the nitrogen atoms at the non-edge sites. However, for the pyridinic-N doped graphene, the adsorption energy does not change significantly as the number of nitrogen atoms increases. Therefore, O2 adsorption and thus the catalytic activity can be enhanced by increasing the number of graphitic-N dopants.

As mentioned above, we found that the possible O2 binding sites are all from the graphene dots doped with three graphitic-N dopants, i.e. 3N-G-E(c), 3N-G-E(s) and 3N-G-M. Understanding the reason would help explore the origin of the ORR activity of the nitrogen doped graphene and benefit the design of high performance ORR catalysts. In Table 2 , we listed the O2 binding energy, CO distance (Å), CO bond order and the total magnetization on the O2 after binding for the case in which O2 binds to the graphene. The binding site locations are highlighted in Fig. 3 and the corresponding optimized structures are shown in Figure S1. The O2 binding energy were calculated to be more negative than −20 kcal/mol and CO bond orders are larger than 0.8. More importantly, the total magnetization of O2 was calculated to be 0–0.5 after binding. Since there are two unpaired electrons on a free O2 molecule, the results indicate that the O2 obtains around one and a half to two electrons from the graphene upon adsorption. Since the pristine graphene is chemically inert, the O2 obtains the electrons from the dopants.

In Table S2, we listed the total magnetization of the ground state for the N-doped graphene quantum dots. The total magnetization for those doped with one graphitic-N (1N-G-E and 1N-G-M) was calculated to be one. After replacing a carbon atom with a graphitic-N dopant, it forms three σ bonds with the neighboring carbon atoms and contributes one p-electron to the π bond. The last valance electron is the extra electrons introduced to the graphene dots. Compared to the graphitic-N doped graphene, no magnetization has been found on any graphene doped with the pyridinic-N dopant at the edge. This is because two of its valance electrons form two σ bonds with the neighboring carbon atoms and one p-electron contributes to the π bond. The last two valance electrons form a lone pair.[21] We have also plotted the PDOSs of the undoped graphene quantum dot, 3N-P-E (ie. three pyridinic-N dopants), 3N-G-M (ie. three graphitic-N dopants) in Figure S2 for comparison. As shown in the figure, the extra electron enhances the magnitude of the states around the Fermi level. Thus, the chemical reducibility of the graphitic-N doped graphene is improved, which favors the transfer of electrons to O2. In comparison, no such enhancement has been found for pyridinic-N doped graphene, as shown in Figure S2. For the O2 binding, the O2 obtains 1.5–2 electrons from the doped graphene, indicating that at least two graphitic-N dopants are required. The lack of excess electrons is the reason why O2 cannot bind on the pure graphene, pyridinic-N doped graphene or the graphene with insufficient dopants, like 1N-G-E and 1N-G-M here. However, as will be discussed in the later sections, having just two graphitic-N dopants is not the sufficient condition. The energetics of the electron transfer to the O2 needs to be analyzed to examine the favorability of O2 binding.

Although 3N-G-E(c), 3N-G-E(s) and 3N-G-M all have three graphitic-N dopants, the number of O2 binding sites on them are different. Only one site was found on 3N-G-E(c) and 3 sites on 3N-G-E(s). 3N-G-M has the most O2 binding sites of 9. This could be attributed to the difference in the local dopant density. 3N-G-E(c) has the highest local dopant density. The neighboring graphitic-N are separated by no more than 2 atoms. Such a high local dopant density creates a very active oxygen binding site, Site 5 in Fig. 1. 3N-G-E(s) has the lowest local dopant density. This also implies that doping configuration may also play a role in determining the number of catalyst active site. More importantly, all the O2 binding sites in Fig. 3 are the carbon atoms bonded to the graphitic-N dopants.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ORR mechanisms of the N-doped graphene dots
ce:section.ce:para: We then simulated the ORR processes on the graphene quantum dots on which the O2 binds. Site 1 on 3N-G-M, Site 3 on 3N-G-E(s) and Site 5 on 3N-G-E(c), which exhibit the most favorable O2 binding energies, were chosen for the study. We focus here on the associative pathway as it has been reported that the associative pathway exhibit a lower activation barriers than the dissociation of *O2.[17,34] In the associative ORR processes, there are three intermediates involved, *OOH, *O, and *OH. To convert the calculated DFT energies into Gibbs free energies, we add the entropic (TS) and zero-point energy (ZPE) corrections to adsorbed species according to Ref.[32,33]. Since we have discussed the O2 binding above, we begin the profiles from the formation of *OOH adsorbent. The energy of the final stage is −5.00 eV, which is twice the calculated Gibbs energy of the formation of a water molecule from H2 and ½O2.

After *O2 binding, we added one electron and one proton to the system and studied the proton binding to both the distal and the proximal oxygen atoms at Site 1 on 3N-G-M and at Site 3 on 3N-G-E(s). We found that the proton is not stable binding to the proximal oxygen and moves to the distal oxygen upon relaxation leading to the formation of the *OOH adsorbent. We continued to add one more electron and one more proton to the system. It is energetically more favorable for the second proton to bind to the distal oxygen, resulting in the OO bond cleavage and the formation of a water molecule. The breaking of the OO bond leads to a 4e− reduction pathway in which the O2 is reduced to two water molecules eventually, as shown in Fig. 4 A and 4B. However, the reduction of *O to *OH and *OH to H2O on 3N-G-E(s) are less favorable than that on 3N-G-M, which could be attributed to the higher chemical activity of edge site carbon atom resulting in stronger *O binding and *OH binding and not favoring their reductions. As shown in Fig. 4, the one-way downward in the free energy profiles of the ORR processes on both graphene dots denote that every ORR step is exothermic and Site 1 on 3N-G-M and Site 3 on 3N-G-E(s) are good ORR catalytic sites. More importantly, no OOH radical or hydrogen peroxide is produced during the whole ORR processes, indicating their high selectivity toward the 4e- reduction pathway.

However, the ORR processes on 3N-G-E(c) at Site 5 is different as shown in Fig. 4C. After O2 binding, we added an electron and tried two cases with the additional proton binding to the proximal and the distal oxygen. In both cases, we found that the hydrogen atom originally bonded to the Site 5 carbon is involved in the ORR processes. If the proton binds to the proximal oxygen, both the CO and the CH bonds break, forming a hydrogen peroxide molecule. However, this process is not energetically favorable. In the other case, if the proton binds to the distal oxygen, we find that the OO bond breaks. The resulting OH radical then bonds with the hydrogen atom originally bonded to Site 5 carbon to form a water molecule. This step is energetically favorable. However, the ORR processes stop at the unfavorable reduction of *O adsorbent due to the strong binding, the CO bond order of which was calculated to be 1.56.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ORR processes on the graphitic-N doped extended graphene
ORR processes on the uniformly doped extended graphene
ce:section.ce:para: We then extended the study from the graphene quantum dots to the extended graphene sheets. As mentioned above, defects like graphitic-N dopants which can introduce extra electrons to the graphene is important for the O2 binding and the ORR activity. Thus, we focus here on the graphitic-N dopants. We doped the graphene uniformly with different number of graphitic-N dopants to explore the effect of the dopant percentage, 1.4% (1N, doped with one graphitic-N), 5.6% (4N, doped with four graphitic-N) and 12.5% (9N, doped with nine graphitic-N), as shown in Fig. 5 . Since the dopants are arranged uniformly, all the graphitic-N dopants are equivalent, and only the C atoms around one N were considered. As shown in Fig. 5, we selected six sites to calculate the O2 binding energy for each case. The sites were chosen according to the distance from the dopants. The adsorption energies were listed in Table 3 . According to their long CO bond distances (around 3.00 Å), no sites on 1N and 4N were found suitable for O2 binding. This is also evident in their weak adsorption energies. However, for 9N, with the nitrogen doping percentage of 12.5%, most of the sites were found suitable for oxygen binding except Site 4. The binding energies are all more negative than −30 kcal/mol with the CO bond distance less than 1.55 Å.

Based on the number of O2 binding sites, we categorized the graphene sheet into three kinds: 1) non-activated graphene on which no sites is suitable for oxygen binding, e.g. 1N and 4N; 2) partially activated graphene on which some carbon atoms bonded to the graphitic-N dopants are suitable for oxygen binding but not all of them (examples of this will be discussed later); 3) fully-activated graphene on which every carbon atoms bonded to the graphitic-N were energetically favorable for O2 binding, e.g. 9N.

As mentioned above, the graphene needs to provide at least two electrons to facilitate the O2 binding. On 4N, there are four graphitic-N dopants and thus four extra electrons. In theory, there are enough electrons for O2 binding on 4N. Yet, no site on 4N has been found suitable for O2 binding. In fact, for the case of the extended graphene, we found that the O2 binding energetics is closely related to spin state energetics. In Table S3, we listed the spin states of the ground states for 1N, 4N and 9N. 1N and 4N exhibit the doublet and the singlet as the ground state, respectively. Thus, one electron spin needs to be flipped for the electron transfer of two electrons to facilitate the O2 binding. We call this energy to flip one electron spin the spin-state energy difference (ΔEdiff), which is the difference between the state with the lowest spin (El, singlet or doublet for graphene doped with an even number or odd number graphitic-N dopant) and the one with second lowest spin (Eh, triplet or quadlet for graphene doped with an even number or odd number graphitic-N dopant), ΔEdiff = Eh – El. We did not calculate the ΔEdiff for 1N as it does not have at least two extra electrons. As listed in Table S3, for 4N, the ΔEdiff is 18.0 kcal/mol, which is large and O2 binding is not favored. However, ΔEdiff decreases to 0.2 kcal/mol when we increased the dopant number to nine (9N). This allows one electron to change its spin readily to facilitate the O2 binding. In fact, the O2 is favorable to bind to all the carbon atoms bonded to the nitrogen. Therefore, as the dopant density increases, the ΔEdiff decreases and the O2 binding is favored.

We then studied the ORR processes. The most favorable O2 binding site of the graphene with 12.5% dopant percentage (Site 6) was selected in this study. The energy profiles are shown in Fig. 6 . After O2 binding, we introduced a H+ and an electron in each ORR step. The H+ was found to be more stable binding to the distal oxygen to form the *OOH adsorbent. Then, we added one proton and one electron to the system. The H+ is again more favorable attaching to the distal oxygen, inducing the cleavage of the OO bond and formation of an adsorbed oxygen atom (*O) and a water molecule. However, the subsequent ORR step, the reduction of *O to *OH, becomes unfavorable. The reaction energy for *O + H+ + e− → *OH is + 0.49 eV. Therefore, the ORR stops after the formation of a water molecule. The whole ORR processes are shown in Fig. 6 Path-1.

However, the ORR processes can restart when a second O2 interacts with the surface. To locate the binding site for the second O2, we selected eleven sites around the *O site to study, as shown in Figure S3. The adsorption energy at each site and the CO distance are listed in Table S4. We removed the water molecule formed in the last step in Path-1 for quicker structural optimizations. For all the sites chosen here, O2 was found to bind to Site 3 only, with the binding energy of −21.1 kcal/mol and a CO distance of 1.46 Å. Then there will be two scenarios for the subsequent ORR processes: 1) the original *O can now be reduced due to the change of its binding strength in the presence of the new adsorbed O2; 2) the original *O remains but affects the reduction of the newly bonded O2. We studied both scenarios and the corresponding energy profiles are shown in Fig. 6 Path-2a and Path-2b. In Path-2a, the reaction energy of *O + H+ + e− → *OH changes from + 0.49 eV to −0.47 eV in the presence of the newly adsorbed O2. The subsequent reduction of *OH to H2O is also favorable. Therefore, the reduction of *O could happen spontaneously with the existence of the second *O2. The ORR reaction scheme for scenario 1 is shown in Scheme 1 A.

For the second scenario, the proton binds to the newly bonded O2 to form *OOH + *O, as shown in Fig. 6 Path-2b. According to our calculation, the formation energy of *OOH + *O is slightly more negative than the formation of *O2 + *OH by −0.11 eV. In this scenario, similar to those in Path-1, the first two steps, the formation of the *OOH adsorbate with the existence of the formerly remained *O and the reduction of *OOH to *O + H2O with the existence of *O, are exothermic. Subsequently, the reduction of *O to *OH is favored by the existence of the remained *O adsorbate, with the reaction energy of + 0.49 eV in Path-1 changed to + 0.06 eV, as shown in Fig. 6 Path-2b. Such a small endothermicity suggests that this 4-electron pathway can still proceed at room temperature. Therefore, *O binding also benefits the reduction of the newly adsorbed O2. The corresponding ORR processes for the second scenario is shown in Scheme 1B.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ORR processes on non-uniformly doped extended graphene
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the limited number of possible uniform doping structures on the graphene model of 72 carbon atoms, we could only study O2 binding and the ORR processes on the 1.4%, 5.6% and 12.5% N-doped graphenes. And only on the graphene with 12.5% nitrogen we found favorable O2 binding sites. To perform more detailed studies of the effects of the doping density, we constructed several non-uniformly N-doped graphenes with different graphitic-N dopant percentages, 3.1%, 4.2%, 6.3% and 8.3%, named as 3N (doped with three graphitic-N), 4N′ (doped with four graphitic-N), 6N (doped with six graphitic-N) and 8N (doped with eight graphitic-N), as shown in Fig. 7 . The non-uniform doping with four graphitic-N is named as ‘4N′’ to distinguish it from the uniform doping configuration 4N mentioned in the last section. Here, we focused on studying the O2 adsorption at carbon atoms bonding to the graphitic-N dopants. We only needed to consider the carbon atoms bonded to one of the nitrogen atoms for the 3N, 4N′ and 8N as the nitrogen atoms are equivalent. For the 6N, every site on the left is equivalent to the corresponding one on the right. Therefore, all the carbon atoms bonded to nitrogen on the left-hand side were studied.

According to the binding energy and CO bond distance listed in Table S5, no site has been found suitable for O2 binding on 3N and 4N′. On 6N, site 2, 3, 3′ and 4 are found favorable for O2 binding. Therefore, 8 of the 18 carbon atoms bonded to the nitrogen atoms in the 6N model are suitable for O2 binding. We consider that the 6N graphene sheet is partially activated. For 8N, O2 can bind to all the carbon atoms that bonded to the graphitic-N dopants. As listed in Table S3, we calculated their ΔEdiff. Except that 8N exhibits a ground state of triplet, the ground states for 3N and 4N′ are their lowest spin states. The energies to change the spin direction for 3N and 4N′ are both more than 15 kcal/mol. Such a large energy does not help O2 binding to the graphene and no sites on 3N and 4N′ were found suitable for O2 binding, which is in agreement with the analysis of ΔEdiff in the uniform doping structures. For 6N, ΔEdiff decreases to 6.0 kcal/mol. Therefore, the 6N graphene is partially activated and the O2 binding sites are located around the middle nitrogen which are affected by the neighbor graphitic-N dopants in favoring the electrons transfer. For a fully-activated graphene on which every carbon atom bonded to the graphitic-N dopants was found suitable for O2 binding, either the increase of the dopant number leads to a negative ΔEdiff, like 8N, or a ΔEdiff close to 0 kcal/mol like 9N. Based on our results, when the dopant percentage of the graphitic-N doped graphene increases to 6.3%, there would be oxygen binding sites. And the lower bound of the graphitic-N dopant percentage for a fully activated N-doped graphene is around 8.3%.

Then we simulated the ORR processes at Site 2 on 6N and Site 2 on 8N. The corresponding energy profiles are shown in Figure S4. In the simulation, the first proton was found to be more favorable to bind to the distal O forming a *OOH adsorbate. Then, the second proton was also more favorable binding to the distal O of the *OOH adsorbate, resulting in the breaking of the OO bond and the formation of *O + H2O. Compared to the ORR processes on 9N, the following step, the reduction of *O to *OH, is slightly favorable for both on 6N and 8N. And the O2 molecule is reduced to two water molecules eventually.

In summary, ab initio study was carried out to investigate O2 adsorption and the ORR processes on the N-doped graphene dots and extended graphene. The main results and conclusions are summarized as follows:
(1)
The adsorption of O2 on the graphitic-N doped graphene quantum dots is more energetically favorable than that on the pyridinic-N doped graphene dots. It is because each graphitic-N dopant introduces one extra electron to the graphene, while the pyridinic-N dopant does not. Upon O2 adsorption, the O2 was found to obtain 1.5–2 electrons from the graphene. Thus, the presence of defects like the graphitic-N dopants that introduces extra electrons to the graphene is important for O2 adsorption.
(2)
For graphene quantum dots, the reaction steps of the ORR processes on 3N-G-M and 3N-G-E(s) are all exothermic. During the reaction, no hydrogen peroxide or OOH radical is formed, indicating the high selectivity towards 4e− reduction pathway of the active sites. However, due to the higher chemical activity of the edge site carbon atom, the reduction of *O to *OH and *OH to H2O on 3N-G-E(s) are less favorable than that on 3N-G-M. In addition, the high graphitic-N dopant density at the edge site could result in poor ORR active site with an unfavorable step of the reduction of *O to *OH, like Site 5 on 3N-G-E(c).
(3)
For O2 adsorption on the extended graphene, we found that there are O2 binding sites as the nitrogen percentage reaches 6.3%. The graphene with the graphitic-N dopant percentage less than 6.3% usually has a ΔEdiff larger than 15 kcal/mol. The energy to flip the electronic spin for electron transfer to the O2 is too high and so O2 binding is not favorable. The lower bound of the graphitic-N dopant percentage for a fully activated N-doped graphene is 8.3%.
(4)
According to the energy profiles of the ORR process on the graphene with 12.5% graphitic-N dopant (9N), the reaction stops at the unfavorable reduction of *O to *OH. However, we identify two subsequent scenarios in which the reduction of oxygen continues to proceed after the binding of a second O2. In the first scenario, we found that the reduction of the original *O becomes favorable to form a water. In the second scenario, the reduction of the newly adsorbed O2 is energetically favorable to form two waters in the presence of an adsorbed *O.
Han Wu: Investigation, Methodology, Writing - original draft. Patrick H.-L. Sit: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge the support by the CityU SRG Funds (Project No. 7004926 and 7005281) and the Research Grants Council of the Hong Kong Special Administrative Region, China (Project No. CityU11304818). This work utilized the High-Performance Computer Cluster managed by the School of Energy and Environment (SEE) of the City University of Hong Kong.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.comptc.2021.113292.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Towards High-Efficiency Nanoelectrocatalysts for Oxygen Reduction through Engineering Advanced Carbon Nanomaterials
